content:
Developers Are Becoming AI Shepherds. That's Not Necessarily a Demotion.
The rapid rise of AI coding tools and autonomous software agents is changing the role of developers in ways that would have seemed improbable just a few years ago. Increasingly, developers are spending less time writing code line by line and more time directing, correcting, and orchestrating AI systems that do the writing for them.
Some see this as the beginning of the end for traditional software engineering. I think it is the beginning of a different kind of engineering.
The word "shepherd" might sound passive, but in reality it describes an increasingly valuable skill. A shepherd is responsible for direction, judgment, quality control, and outcomes. AI agents can generate thousands of lines of code in minutes, but they still lack business context, long-term architectural thinking, accountability, and the intuition that experienced engineers develop over years.
The bottleneck is no longer typing, but deciding. Today's best developers are already discovering that their value lies in asking the right questions, defining clear objectives, breaking problems into manageable tasks, and evaluating the output of multiple AI agents. In many organizations, productivity is shifting from "How fast can you code?" to "How well can you manage an AI workforce?"
That shift mirrors previous industrial revolutions. Construction workers did not disappear when excavators arrived. Farmers did not become obsolete when tractors replaced horses. The tools became exponentially more capable, while the humans moved into roles that required oversight, planning, and decision-making.
In essence, software development appears to be following the same path.
This also explains why companies are racing to own AI-native development environments. Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Gemini Code Assist, and other platforms are evolving beyond code completion. They are becoming operating systems for autonomous software engineering, where multiple agents collaborate on writing code, reviewing pull requests, generating documentation, testing applications, fixing bugs, and even deploying to production.
In that environment, the developer becomes less of a coder and more of a conductor leading an orchestra of specialized AI systems.
However, this transition will not benefit everyone equally.
Developers whose expertise is limited to writing routine CRUD applications or repetitive boilerplate may face increasing pressure as AI becomes capable of producing comparable work in seconds. Meanwhile, engineers who understand distributed systems, cybersecurity, databases, infrastructure, product design, compliance, and business strategy will become even more valuable because AI still depends on humans to define goals and verify outcomes.
The next decade may also see the emergence of entirely new roles. AI workflow architects, agent supervisors, prompt engineers for enterprise systems, AI software auditors, autonomous system trainers, and AI governance specialists could become as common as frontend or backend developers are today.
Perhaps the most overlooked implication is that the number of software creators may explode. As AI lowers the technical barrier, entrepreneurs, designers, scientists, lawyers, and healthcare professionals will increasingly build software themselves by directing AI agents. Professional developers will then be called upon not merely to write code, but to ensure these AI-generated systems are scalable, secure, maintainable, and compliant.
In that future, coding becomes a smaller part of software engineering and thinking becomes the larger one.
The developer who thrives will not necessarily be the one who writes the most code. It will be the one who can manage teams of AI agents, understand complex systems, make sound engineering decisions, and translate business problems into autonomous workflows.
Developers are becoming managers of intelligence. History suggests that those who learn to direct powerful tools rarely become less valuable. They simply stop measuring their contribution by the number of lines they write and start measuring it by the value their systems create. Hey developers, how advanced are you at shepherding your AI agent/s?
